cataloging_subject.label: Everyman
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The anonymous Middle English morality commonly called Everyman, treated as one intellectual work. Four early printed states survive in five known physical objects because the earliest Pynson state is divided between the separately preserved Douce and Bandinel fragments. Those print objects, A. C. Cawley's 1961 edition, the U-M/OTA encodings, modern scholarly editions, and facsimiles are transmission layers rather than separate works. The eighteen DIV1 elements and supplied scene headings in the repository XML are editorial navigation imposed by Ian Lancashire, not eighteen constituent works or an authorial scene structure.

catalog_summary.title: Everyman
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous English translator-adapter
catalog_summary.date: Late fifteenth or early sixteenth century; exact date unknown; earliest surviving Pynson state dated c. 1515 by current USTC and c. 1518–1519 by Freeman/EMOTHE
catalog_summary.region: England; translated and adapted from the Middle Dutch Elckerlijc tradition of the Low Countries

notes: Normalized discovery title. The early Skot title pages carry a much longer descriptive title, and the opening messenger names the work The Somonynge of Eueryman.
Catalog spelling normalized from varying early forms; XML line 4 reads ‘The Somonynge of Eueryman.’

titles.value: A treatyse how the hye fader of heven sendeth dethe to somon every creature to come and gyve a counte of theyr lyves in this worlde and is in maner of a morall playe
notes: A normalized transcription of the descriptive title used for the complete Skot printings; lineation and letterforms differ between carrier and catalog display.
Current scholarship regards the English work as a translation and adaptation of Elckerlijc, but neither the English translator nor the exact Dutch exemplar is known.
The adaptation role records substantive English reshaping as well as translation; it is not a claim for a second person.

date_statements.display: Late fifteenth or early sixteenth century; exact date unknown
notes: The repository XML and OTA replacement repeat 1485 as a creation date, but the reviewed sources do not establish that exact date. It is therefore retained only as a legacy transmission assertion and not normalized as the work's composition date.

date_statements.display: Earliest surviving Richard Pynson state: c. 1515 under current USTC dating, or c. 1518–1519 in Freeman/EMOTHE; exact date uncertain
notes: The earliest state (STC 10604 / USTC 501308) survives in two detached physical fragments: four Douce leaves preserving lines 684–922 and two Bandinel leaves preserving lines 429–552, together 325 lines. Current USTC dates the state c. 1515 and lists only the Bodleian copy; Freeman and EMOTHE date it c. 1518–1519 and supply the separately held Bandinel evidence. No singular lineage binding is used for this state-level date.
This identifies the English linguistic and early print context, not a securely documented city or place of composition.
The association belongs to the Middle Dutch source tradition; it does not relocate the anonymous English adaptation.

language_statements.label: Latin

scope.description: Brief liturgical or biblical phrases in XML lines 886–887 and 915

notes: Three FOREIGN elements encode ‘In manus tuas,’ ‘Commendo spiritum meum,’ and ‘Ite, maledicti, in ignem eternum.’

form_statements.description: A 921-line dramatic verse text with brief prose rubrics, stage directions, and a printer's colophon. Those paratextual prose features do not make the work mixed-form.

genre_statements.label: Morality play
notes: The early descriptive title says the treatise is ‘in maner of a morall playe.’ No performance is recorded before 1901, so the label does not itself prove medieval stage performance.

genre_statements.label: Devotional literature
notes: The early title calls it a treatise, and modern scholarship emphasizes its compatibility with late-medieval reading about dying and the spiritual life.

subject_statements.label: The transience and desertion of worldly attachments

summaries.value: God sends Death to summon Everyman to a final reckoning. Fellowship, Kindred, Cousin, and Goods refuse his journey; Good Deeds is initially too weak to accompany him, but Knowledge leads him through Confession and penance until Good Deeds can rise. Beauty, Strength, Discretion, and Five Wits pledge aid but abandon him at the grave. Only Good Deeds enters with him, while Knowledge remains to hear the angels welcome his soul. A Doctor's epilogue directs hearers to reject pride and prepare their accounts before death. The English work translates and reshapes the Middle Dutch Elckerlijc into an allegorical meditation on mortality, sacramental preparation, charity, and salvation.

This record describes the anonymous work, not a single early print, Cawley's 1961 edited carrier, or the electronic encoding. The repository XML's eighteen DIV1 elements and visible ‘Scene’ headings are supplied modern navigation. Scene 4 is absent, numbering runs from 1 to 19, and the first DIV1 also contains the inherited label ‘French scene’; these encoding artifacts are not used as the work's intrinsic content hierarchy.

extent.description: One 921-line dramatic verse work, from the Messenger's prologue through the Doctor's epilogue, plus brief prose title rubric, stage directions, FINIS, and Skot colophon. The repository XML has 18 supplied DIV1 scene containers, 19 HEAD elements, 921 numbered L elements, 211 speeches, 28 page milestones, nine signature milestones, four STAGE elements, and three FOREIGN elements. Those encoding counts do not redefine the work as 18 scenes or as mixed prose and verse.

content_parts.label: The Somonyng of Everyman
content_parts.summary: Complete dramatic verse from the Messenger's prologue, through Death's summons and Everyman's failed and successful searches for companions, to his death, reception by the Angel, and the Doctor's moral epilogue.

locators.value: 19 and every odd PDF page through 79 (printed pp. 17–77); PDF p. 17 is the Huth title image

notes: The METS edition numbers an added terminal Amen as line 922; the repository/Cawley sequence has 921 numbered verse lines. This is an editorial lineation difference, not evidence of a second work.

related_works.title: Elckerlijc
notes: Current scholarship treats Everyman as both translation and adaptation of the Middle Dutch work. The exact Dutch text used by the English translator is unknown and apparently not extant; this relation is between works, not a claim that a specific surviving Elckerlijc print was copied directly.

evidence.citation: General Corpus pinned CME source file Everyman.xml, CME commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c
evidence.summary: Primary repository evidence for title, anonymous responsibility, U-M and OTA identifiers, 1993 electronic publication, Cawley 1961 source statement, omitted apparatus, supplied scene divisions, legacy 1485 date, complete text, lineation, speakers, Latin passages, and colophon.

evidence.citation: Local structural inspection of the pinned Everyman.xml
evidence.summary: Counted 18 DIV1, 19 HEAD, 921 L, 28 PB, 211 SP/SPEAKER, four STAGE, nine signature MILESTONE, and three FOREIGN elements; verified missing supplied Scene 4 and the inherited first-DIV label ‘French scene.’

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections, ‘Everyman,’ Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse
evidence.summary: Current official discovery and reading page for the U-M item; identifies Anonymous, Manchester University Press 1961, OTA U-1350-A, public access, and current No Copyright–United States assertion. Its contents list also exposes the supplied scene-number gap.

evidence.citation: Clifford Davidson, Martin W. Walsh, and Ton J. Broos, eds., Everyman and Its Dutch Original, Elckerlijc (Kalamazoo: Medieval Institute Publications, 2007)
evidence.locator: Introduction, especially pp. 1–11; Early Editions; English text and textual notes
evidence.summary: Scholarly evidence for the translation-adaptation relationship to Elckerlijc, unknown exact Dutch exemplar, ambiguous reading/performance status, early-print census as known in 2007, Huth-base editorial method, comparison witnesses, textual variants, form, genre, themes, and lineation. It predates the reported Bandinel Q1 leaves.

Scholarly repertory record identifying the anonymous English morality as 921 verse lines, listing four early print states with USTC and ESTC identifiers, and reporting no manuscript witness for the English work. Its state-level census does not establish that each state survives in only one physical object.

evidence.citation: Universal Short Title Catalogue records 501308, 501897, 502061, and 502713
evidence.summary: Current bibliographic records for the two incomplete Pynson and two complete Skot print states, with revised STC and ESTC numbers, approximate dates, holding institutions, shelfmarks where supplied, format, and subscription EEBO links. USTC 501308 records the Bodleian survivor but does not separately locate the Bandinel leaves of the same state.

evidence.citation: W. W. Greg and John S. Farmer, Everyman: A Facsimile Reproduction of the Editions of John Skot and Richard Pynson (London, 1912), Internet Archive scan
evidence.locator: Complete Huth/Skot section and British Museum Pynson fragment
evidence.summary: Complete public scan of the 1912 facsimile volume, reproducing the complete British Library Huth 32 Skot print and the surviving latter two-thirds of British Library C.21.c.17 printed by Pynson; visually confirms early titles, text, colophons, and carrier boundaries.

evidence.citation: University of Oxford, The History of the Book in Britain, ‘Everyman and Elckerlijc’
evidence.summary: University research resource situating the English translation in the early-sixteenth-century print tradition and the Dutch source work in the late fifteenth century; used only for broad chronology and cross-cultural context.

evidence.citation: Arthur Freeman, ‘Everyman and Others, Part II: The Bandinel Fragments,’ The Library 9, no. 4 (December 2008): 397–427
evidence.locator: Abstract and article bibliographic record; Everyman fragment discussion
evidence.summary: Peer-reviewed report of two newly recovered leaves from the first Pynson edition of Everyman. Later EMOTHE analysis identifies them as signatures B3–B4, lines 429–552, separate from the Douce fragment; the discovery postdates Cawley's 1961 edition.

evidence.citation: Jesús Tronch Pérez, ed., The Summoning of Everyman: A Critical, Modernized Edition Based on the Third Quarto (Valencia: ARTELOPE–EMOTHE, Universitat de València, 2019; text-only edition first published 2018), EMOTHE 0460
evidence.locator: TEI header, listWit, edition introduction, and critical apparatus
evidence.summary: Complete public TEI critical modernized edition based on Q3, with Q1–Q4 witness descriptions and encoded variants. Its introduction distinguishes the Douce and Bandinel physical fragments of Q1, maps them to lines 684–922 and 429–552, and reports 325 surviving Q1 lines in all; its Q1 dating of c. 1518–1519 differs from current USTC. The embedded Zenodo DOI did not resolve during review, but the live EMOTHE page and exact XML remained accessible.

evidence.citation: Everyman source-lineage manifest, Cawley edition and early-print evidence
evidence.summary: Linked evidence for the immediate Cawley 1961 source edition and its relationship to the U-M/OTA encoding.

open_questions.question: When and where was the English translation-adaptation composed, and which lost or surviving Dutch state supplied its exact exemplar?
next_steps: Retain a broad late-fifteenth/early-sixteenth-century date rather than the XML's unsupported exact 1485.
Review new textual scholarship on English–Dutch direction, rhyme evidence, and the chronology of Elckerlijc prints.

open_questions.question: How should the competing c. 1515 USTC and c. 1518–1519 Freeman/EMOTHE dates for Q1 be resolved, and will future catalog revisions alter the dating, ordering, or STC mapping of the four early English print states?
next_steps: Prefer current USTC/ESTC identifiers while preserving the different Freeman/EMOTHE and 2007 METS chronologies in notes.
Recheck USTC and holding-library catalogs during future manifest review, including whether USTC 501308 can represent both detached Q1 fragments.

notes: Cawley's modern editorial work and the historical English work have different rights statuses; see the linked lineage manifest.

No English manuscript witness is known. Four early print states survive in five physical objects: three fragmentary objects (Douce Q1, Bandinel Q1, and British Library Q2) and two complete Skot copies.

The cached Greg/Farmer facsimile supplies direct images of Huth 32 and British Library C.21.c.17, but not the Huntington/Britwell Skot copy that Cawley identifies as his base or either physical fragment of the earliest Pynson state.
